coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen–silicon Co-doped carbon dots synthesized based on lemon peel for copper (II) detection via a dynamic quenching mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Jiaxue
Wu, Li
Zhu, Yuxin
Xiong, Zhili
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: As a metal, copper plays an important role in biological systems. However, excessive intake can lead to toxicity and disease. Therefore, there is a need to establish accurate and sensitive methods to detect and monitor copper levels in food and the environment. Herein, nitrogen-silicon co-doped carbon dots (N,Si-CDs) were synthesized using lemon peel as the biomass precursor by one-step hydrothermal synthesis with the addition of citric acid solution and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES). The prepared N,Si-CDs showed excellent photobleaching resistance, brilliant fluorescence performance and good selectivity. The experiments showed that the fluorescence of N,Si-CDs decreased after the interaction of N,Si-CDs with copper ions. Further studies showed that the decrease in fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe due to Cu2+ was dynamic quenching. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of copper ions. The linear range was 0.5 μM–250 μM, the R 2 was 0.995, and the limit of detection was 0.43 μM. Finally, the method has been successfully applied to the detection of copper ions in the water environment and food, and the recoveries of 99.4%–102.1 % were obtained, which indicated that the method had a promising future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass precursor
Nitrogen-silicon co-doped carbon dots
Copper ions
Dynamic quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of N,Si-CDs by Cu2+
Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples
Optimization of detection conditions for N,Si-CDs
Selectivity to Cu2+ and anti-interference of N,Si-CDs
Mechanism for the detection of Cu2+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University
Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image and the HRTEM image of the N,Si-CDs. b The size distribution of the N,Si-CDs. c The FT-IR of the N,Si-CDs.

UV–vis spectra of N,Si-CDs, excitation and emission spectra of N,Si-CDs. b Emission spectra of N,Si-CDs for varying excitation wavelengths. c The influence of UV irradiation duration on fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs.
The effect of the reaction time on N,Si-CDs stability. b The effect of the temperature on N,Si-CDs stability.
a The fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs with different concentrations of Cu2+. b Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cu2+(0.5 μM–250 μM).

a Selectivity of N,Si-CDs for assaying Cu2+; b Interference of the presence of different substances on the fluorescence intensity of the N,Si-CDs/Cu2+ system. F0 was the fluorescence intensity of the N,Si-CDs not mixed with analytes and F was the fluorescence intensity of the N,Si-CDs mixed with different analytes. In both experiments, The concentration of Cu2+ was 0.25 mM, the concentration of different interfering substances was 5 mM.

a UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,Si-CDs, Cu2+ and N,Si-CDs + Cu2+ and the emission spectrum of N,Si-CDs at λex = 370 nm b The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N,Si-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+.
The recovery rate of Cu2+ at 3 concentration levels in water, milk, and grape juice (n = 3).
Detection of Cu2+ in different literature.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen–silicon Co-doped carbon dots synthesized based on lemon peel for copper (II) detection via a dynamic quenching mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, China.
Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a metal, copper plays an important role in biological systems. However, excessive intake can lead to toxicity and disease. Therefore, there is a need to establish accurate and sensitive methods to detect and monitor copper levels in food and the environment. Herein, nitrogen-silicon co-doped carbon dots (N,Si-CDs) were synthesized using lemon peel as the biomass precursor by one-step hydrothermal synthesis with the addition of citric acid solution and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES). The prepared N,Si-CDs showed excellent photobleaching resistance, brilliant fluorescence performance and good selectivity. The experiments showed that the fluorescence of N,Si-CDs decreased after the interaction of N,Si-CDs with copper ions. Further studies showed that the decrease in fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe due to Cu2+ was dynamic quenching. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of copper ions. The linear range was 0.5 μM–250 μM, the R 2 was 0.995, and the limit of detection was 0.43 μM. Finally, the method has been successfully applied to the detection of copper ions in the water environment and food, and the recoveries of 99.4%–102.1 % were obtained, which indicated that the method had a promising future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a vital nutrient for an organism, Copper is involved in many biological processes including tumor immunity [1], cellular apoptosis, angiogenesis [2], and the elimination of toxic free radicals [3]. The recommended daily allowance (RDA) of copper for adults is 900 μg [4], but the daily intake from food is sufficient, therefore excess copper left in food or the environment can be harmful. Excessive intake of Cu2+ can lead to hearing loss [5,6], neurological disorders [7,8] and even cancer [9,10].

Therefore, it is crucial to accurately detect Cu2+ in food and the environment. Currently, the traditional methods for detecting Cu2+ include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [11] and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12], which have good selectivity and sensitivity but are limited in their application due to the large size of the instruments required. Later, electrochemical methods [13], fluorescence immunochromatographic assay [14], surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectrometry (SERS) [15] and colorimetric methods [16] were developed, but they also have shortcomings such as expensive materials required, complicated detection processes, and poor stability.

Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon quantum dots, are usually defined as carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm. In 2006, Sun [17] et al. prepared fluorescent nanoparticles with good light-emitting properties for the first time by laser etching, which were officially named “carbon quantum dots”. In recent years, fluorescence analysis methods based on CDs have attracted extensive attention due to their advantages such as cheapness, ease of operation and visualization [18,19]. For example, Lin [20] et al. prepared TO-CDs to detect Cu2+ ions in soil. Similarly, Omer [21] synthesized phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped CDs to assay Cu2+ ions. Han [22] et al. reported CDs via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) for the detection of Cu2+.

The precursors of carbon quantum dots are divided into inorganic and organic carbon sources. Inorganic carbon sources are usually graphite and carbon nanotubes, which are not environmentally friendly, complicated to synthesize and consume a lot of energy. Organic carbon sources have been widely studied due to their wide range of sources and low prices. Sahu [23] et al. synthesized highly photoluminescent carbon dots for the first time by a one-step hydrothermal method using biomass orange juice. Since then, researchers have started to focus on biomass waste, such as guava leaves [24], cherry tomato stalks [25], peanut shells [26] and rice straw [27].

Heteroatom-doped CDs often exhibit low toxicity, good fluorescence activity and catalytic activity compared to bare CDs [28]. To further improve the properties of the CDs, two or more different atoms can be doped, which is called co-doping. He [29] et al. have prepared high quantum yield Si, N-codoped carbon dots (SiNCDs) to detect Fe3+ in human serum. The synergistic effect between the doped heteroatoms in the carbon dots can create a unique electronic structure and obtain better performance, thus it has a wide range of applications.

In this work, nitrogen-silicon co-doped carbon dots (N,Si-CDs) were successfully prepared by the straightforward hydrothermal method. The doping of N and Si atoms was employed to improve the electronic properties and the surface chemical activity of the CDs to a great extent. The prepared N,Si-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties and optical stability. The fluorescence intensity of the N,Si-CDs produced a satisfactory linear relationship with the concentration of copper ions in the linear range of 0.5 μM–250 μM, with a LOD of 0.43 μM. The sensor constructed by N,Si-CDs has high selectivity and resistance to photobleaching and has been successfully applied to the determination of copper ions in the water environment and food, which also indicates that the sensor has a better application prospect.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with a JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). A Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Co, USA) was applied to measure the Fourier transform infrared spectra. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by an Escalab250Xi (Thermo Fisher Co, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured by a TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd, China). The fluorescence was analyzed on an F380 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Sci. &Tech. Co., Ltd, China). Time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (PLS 980, Edinburgh, UK) was used to measure fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield. A PHS-3W pH meter (Shanghai Leici Co., Ltd, China) was used for pH measurement. Zhongjia LC-400 Low-speed centrifuge (Anhui Zhongke Zhongjia Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd, China), HN202 Thermostatic electric drying oven (Nantong Hunan Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd, China), KQ5200 ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd, China), Vortex-1 Vortex Mixer (Shanghai Huxi Industry Co., Ltd., China) were used for synthesis of N,Si-CDs.

3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), citric acid solution 6 % (w/v) in H2O, glucose, lactose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, l-aspartic acid, alanine, cysteine and lysine were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CaCl2, ZnCl2, NaCl, KCl, K2SO4, K3PO4, MgCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, Pb(NO3)3, Cr(NO3)3 and As2O3 were purchased from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The lemons, milk and grape juice were purchased from the local market (Shenyang, China).

ce:list-item.ce:para: 1g of lemon peel was added into a total of 4 mL of APTES and 5 mL of citric acid solution. The solution, held for 30s on the vortex mixer and sonicated for 30s to favor the solvent dispersion, was added into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated in an oven at 180°C for 6h. Afterward, the autoclave was kept out of the oven and cooled to room temperature. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 5min. Finally, the N,Si-CDs stock solution was obtained after filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter, dialyzed for 24h with 1000 MWCO, and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:para:
Quantum yield (QY) is a significant factor in analyzing fluorescent materials. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of N,Si-CDs was measured and calculated with the following equation:
A b s − Q Y =
L
s a m p l e
E
r e f e r e n c e
−
E
s a m p l e
where Abs-QY was the quantum yield, L
sample was the emission intensity, and E
reference and E
sample were the intensities of the excitation light not absorbed by the reference sample and the sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of N,Si-CDs by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The N,Si-CDs stock solution was diluted 50-fold with ultrapure water and 500 μL was added to a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube, followed by 300 μL of various concentrations of Cu2+ standard solution (0–400 μM) and 200 μL of ultrapure water to the centrifuge tube. The solution was vortexed for 1 min and incubated at 45°C for 5 min. The fluorescence spectra of the solutions containing different concentrations of Cu2+ were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

To study the selectivity of N,Si-CDs and to exclude the interference of other substances on the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs, several potentially interfering substances such as biomolecules (ascorbic acid (AA), lactose, uric acid (UA), glucose (Glu), cysteine (Cys), aspartic acid (Asp), lysine (Lys) and alanine (Ala)), cations (Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cr3+ and As3+) and anions (SO4 2− and PO4 3−) were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the practicability of this method, spiked recovery experiments were conducted on Cu2+ in water, milk, and juice. All samples were obtained from the school supermarket. Simple pretreatment was performed, the specific steps were as follows: the milk and juice were diluted samples 20-fold and centrifuged for 20 min at 5000 rpm. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filters. Solutions of different concentrations of Cu2+ (10 μM、50 μM、200 μM) were then prepared by adding Cu2+ to water, milk and fruit juice. 500 μL of N,Si-CDs solution and 200 μL of water were taken in a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube, and 300 μL Cu2+ (10 μM、50 μM、200 μM) was added. Then the fluorescence spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm.
For all the experiments, the values are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Statistical analyses were performed using Microsoft Excel.

To study the morphology and size distribution of the N,Si-CDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used to characterize the morphology of N,Si-CDs particles. Fig. 1 a displayed that the N,Si-CDs were well dispersed and the particle size distribution was relatively well uniform. In addition, The HRTEM image (insert of Fig. 1a) showed that the N,Si-CDs had a clear parallel crystal structure and the lattice plane of the CQDs was 0.25 nm, which was similar to the (100) graphite facets of graphitic carbon [30]. The histogram of their particle size distribution was shown in Fig. 1b, and the average particle size of N,Si-CDs was 4.15 nm.

The functional groups on the surface of the N,Si-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1c). The peaks at 3351 cm−1, 2931 cm−1, 1652 cm−1, and 1386 cm−1 were assigned as the stretching vibration of N–H, C–H, CO, and C–N respectively [31]. The peak at 1569 cm−1 was caused by wagging vibrations of N–H. The strong band located at 1115 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of Si-OR [32]. These groups demonstrated that nitrogen-silicon co-doped carbon dots have been obtained. Meanwhile, XPS was used to determine the element compositions and surface states of N,Si-CDs. Fig. S1a showed four obvious peaks in the XPS of N,Si-CDs, which correspond to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Si 2p, respectively. Four peaks at 284.3, 286.1, 287.3 and 288.4 eV in the spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S1b) indicated the primary forms of C–C/CC/C–Si, C–O/C–N, C–O–C and O–CO [29,33,34]. The N1s region (Fig. S1c) showed four different peaks at 398.4, 399.5400.1 and 400.7 eV, which correspond to N–Si, N–C, (C)3–N and N–H, respectively [35,36]. O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1d) showed three peaks centered at 530.9, 531.2, and 532.9 eV corresponding to Si–O, CO and C–O, respectively [34,37]. Two peaks at 101.8 and 102.3 eV in the Si 2p region (Fig. S1e) corresponded to Si–C and Si–N [37]. In addition, It was detected that the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield of the prepared N,Si-CDs at a wavelength of 370 nm was 5.88 %.

The optical properties of N,Si-CDs were explored by ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a UV–Vis spectra showed that N,Si-CDs had a small UV absorption peak near 241 nm, which was attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2b, When the excitation wavelength is between 300 nm and 440 nm, the peak position of the emission peak is almost unchanged. The fluorescence spectra also demonstrated an optimal emission wavelength at 458 nm for N,Si-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

To test the photobleaching resistance of N,Si-CDs, the fluorescence intensity changes of N,Si-CDs were examined under 365 nm UV light irradiation for 60 min. As shown in Fig. 2c–N,Si-CDs showed light blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The change of fluorescence intensity with the increase of UV irradiation duration was small, indicating that N,Si-CDs can maintain their fluorescence properties for a certain time, and have resistance to photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for N,Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of reaction time and temperature on the detection of Cu2+ by N,Si-CDs were also investigated. After adding 400 μM Cu2+ to N,Si-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs was observed to change within 40 min. The results were shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs was the lowest at 5 min, so 5 min was the optimal reaction time. The fluorescence intensity changes of N,Si-CDs were also investigated after adding 350 μM Cu2+ for 5 min at 25°C–55 °C. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase in temperature, and the fluorescence intensity was unchanged after 45 °C, so 45 °C was finally chosen as the reaction temperature.

The fluorescence spectra of N,Si-CDs were measured at increasing concentrations of Cu2+ under optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs showed a regular decrease after the addition of Cu2+ as shown in Fig. 4 a, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of N,Si-CDs was mainly affected by the concentration of Cu2+. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs and the concentration of Cu2+ in solution was explored. As shown in Fig. 4b, the linear regression equation was: y = −2.877x+1972.453, R 2 = 0.995 (where y is the fluorescence intensity of N,Si-CDs and x is the concentration of Cu2+), and the LOD was 0.43 μM, which was much lower than the standards for Drinking Water Quality (GB5749-2022) acceptable limits (15.74 μM for Cu2+).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity to Cu2+ and anti-interference of N,Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of N,Si-CDs to Cu2+, different kinds of interfering substances including ascorbic acid (AA), lactose, uric acid (UA), glucose (Glu), cysteine (Cys), aspartic acid (Asp), lysine (Lys) and alanine (Ala), cations (Ca2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cr3+ and As3+) and anions (SO4 2− and PO4 3−) were added to the N,Si-CDs system under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 , only Cu2+ could cause a sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity, whereas no significant fluorescence changes were observed in the presence of other interfering substances, which indicated that the N,Si-CDs had a high selectivity for the detection of Cu2+, which proved its potential for future applications. In the interference experiment, different substances were added to the N,Si-CDs/Cu2+ system to investigate anti-interference of N,Si-CDs. The interference experiment (Fig. 5b) indicated that most of these substances and ions had no significant influence on the detection of Cu2+. Consequently, the sensing system showed satisfactory anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Quenching mechanisms generally include static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [38]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, comparing the UV–Vis spectra of N,Si-CDs and N,Si-CDs + Cu2+, it could be seen that the UV absorptions of Cu2+(250 μM) and N,Si-CDs + Cu2+ were approximately the same, suggesting that new compounds were not generated. In addition, the emission spectra of N,Si-CDs did not overlap with the absorption spectra of Cu2+, suggesting that the quenching mechanism was not FRET or IFE. Fluorescence lifetime is the primary parameter for determining the fluorescent quenching mechanism. One of the differences between static and dynamic quenching is whether the lifetime of the CD changes in the absence and presence of the quenching agent. Fig. 6b showed the fluorescence decay curves of N,Si-CDs before and after treatment with Cu2+. The average lifetime of N,Si-CDs was calculated to be 6.08 ns. After the addition of Cu2+ (250 μM) the average lifetime of N,Si-CDs was calculated to be 5.25 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime of N,Si-CDs was significantly shortened. This indicated that the decrease in fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe caused by Cu2+ belongs to dynamic quenching.

To investigate the practical application of N,Si-CDs for the detection of Cu2+, water, milk, and grape juice were selected for the spiking experiments, and the results are shown in Table 1 . The spiked recoveries in the samples were in the range of 99.4%–102.1 % with the RSD≤3.7 % (n = 3), which indicated that the prepared fluorescent probes for N,Si-CDs are relatively reliable and can be applied to the actual samples for analysis, and have good prospects of being applied to environmental and food monitoring. Meanwhile, this work was also compared with the detection methods reported in the literature, and the results are presented in Table 2 . Compared with other methods, this method utilized the bio-waste lemon peel as a raw material, which is more environmentally friendly. As can be seen from Table 2, this method has a wide linear range and a low detection limit, which shows good prospects for application.

In this study, a novel fluorescent probe co-modified with nitrogen and silicon using lemon peel as a biomass precursor was synthesized. Heteroatom-doped CDs have good fluorescent properties chemical properties. It can be used for the quantitative detection of copper ions in water environments and food by decreasing the fluorescence intensity through dynamic quenching after reacting with copper ions. The N,Si-CDs showed good fluorescence response and strong resistance to photobleaching. Under the optimal experimental conditions, a good linear relationship was established. The detection range is 0.5 μM–250 μM while the detection limit is 0.43 μM. With a wide linear range, low detection limit and easy operation, this method is reliable and efficient which provides a new idea for clinical analysis.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Not applicable.
The datasets generated during and analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Jiaxue Su: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Li Wu: Software, Data curation. Yuxin Zhu: Methodology, Investigation. Zhili Xiong: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Longshan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).

Chem.--Eur. J.
